questions.question: Explain the Producer-Consumer problem with a bounded buffer. What synchronization primitives are required?
keyPoints: Producer generates data and stores in buffer
Consumer removes and processes data from buffer
Buffer has finite capacity N
Requires counting semaphores: empty (initially N) and full (initially 0)
Requires binary semaphore mutex for mutual exclusion
Potential issue: race condition without proper synchronization

questions.question: What is the Readers-Writers problem? Differentiate between reader-preference and writer-preference solutions.
keyPoints: Multiple readers can access simultaneously, writers need exclusive access
Reader-preference: new readers can join even if writer waiting, may cause writer starvation
Writer-preference: no new readers allowed when writer waiting, may cause reader starvation
Solutions require tracking readcount and using semaphores like mutex and rw_mutex

questions.question: Explain the Dining Philosophers problem. How can deadlock occur in this scenario?
keyPoints: Five philosophers, five forks, each needs two forks to eat
Philosopher alternates between thinking and eating
Deadlock occurs when each picks up left fork simultaneously (circular wait)
All hold one fork waiting for another held by neighbor
Solutions: break symmetry, resource hierarchy, allow philosopher to pick only if both forks available

questions.question: What is the Sleeping Barber problem? How does it relate to producer-consumer synchronization?
keyPoints: Barber shop with one barber and limited waiting chairs
Barber sleeps when no customers, wakes when customer arrives
Customer leaves if all waiting chairs occupied
Related to producer-consumer: customers are items produced, barber consumes (services) them
Uses counting semaphore for customers and mutex for chair access

questions.question: Define race condition and critical section. How do synchronization mechanisms prevent race conditions?
keyPoints: Race condition: outcome depends on timing of interleaved operations
Critical section: code segment accessing shared resources
Mutual exclusion: only one process in critical section at a time
Progress: processes not in remainder can participate in decision
Bounded waiting: limit on number of times others can enter after request
Synchronization primitives (semaphores, locks, monitors) enforce these properties

questions.question: What is the Cigarette Smokers problem? Explain how synchronization solves it.
keyPoints: Three smokers each have one ingredient (tobacco, paper, matches)
Agent places two ingredients on table, smoker with third ingredient takes them
Problem demonstrates conditional synchronization and signaling
Each smoker waits for the two ingredients they lack
Requires three smokers to wait on different conditions, agent signals appropriate smoker
Demonstrates use of individual semaphores or condition variables per smoker

questions.question: Explain the three conditions necessary for deadlock in the context of classical synchronization problems.
keyPoints: Mutual exclusion: resources cannot be shared (forks, buffer slots, chairs)
Hold and wait: processes hold allocated resources while waiting for others
No preemption: resources cannot be forcibly taken away
Circular wait:形成等待环 (waiting cycle) - philosopher i waits for philosopher i+1
Breaking any one condition prevents deadlock

questions.question: Write and explain the pseudocode for the Producer-Consumer problem using semaphores. Also discuss initialization values and potential race conditions if semaphores are misused.
keyPoints: Initialize empty = N (buffer size), full = 0, mutex = 1
Producer: wait(empty), wait(mutex), add item, signal(mutex), signal(full)
Consumer: wait(full), wait(mutex), remove item, signal(mutex), signal(empty)
Order matters: wait(empty) before mutex to prevent deadlock
Race condition if mutex not used: both producers/consumers may access buffer simultaneously
Buffer overflow if empty check skipped, underflow if full check skipped
Explain with example scenarios showing correct/incorrect execution

questions.question: Write and explain a solution to the Dining Philosophers problem that prevents both deadlock and starvation. Include pseudocode for philosopher behavior.
keyPoints: Use semaphore solution with asymmetric picking (odd/even philosopher)
OR use resource hierarchy: always pick lower-numbered fork first
Initialize forks as mutex semaphores or use a state array
Philosopher states: thinking, hungry, eating
Test and set operations to check if neighbors are eating
Hungry philosopher can eat only if both neighbors not eating
This solution guarantees no deadlock (breaks circular wait) and no starvation (fair scheduling)
Provide pseudocode showing pick_up and put_down functions with tests

questions.question: Explain the Readers-Writers problem with writer-priority. Write pseudocode for reader and writer processes and explain how it prevents writer starvation.
keyPoints: Writer-priority ensures writers do not starve
Readers must check if a writer is waiting before entering
Writer signals waiting flag, readers check this flag
If writer waiting, new readers block themselves
Mutex protects readcount, rw_mutex protects actual data
Writer: wait(wrt) exclusively, then signal(wrt) when done
Reader: wait(mutex), increment readcount, if first reader wait(rw_mutex), signal(mutex), read, wait(mutex), decrement readcount, if last reader signal(rw_mutex), signal(mutex)
Additional writeTry semaphore or queue to give writers priority

questions.question: Explain the Sleeping Barber problem with N waiting chairs. Write pseudocode for barber and customer processes and discuss synchronization issues.
keyPoints: Initialize: customers = 0, mutex = 1, waiting = N, barberReady = 0
Customer: wait(mutex), if waiting > 0 then waiting--, signal(barberReady), signal(mutex), wait(customers)
Barber: while true: wait(barberReady), wait(mutex), waiting++, signal(mutex), signal(customers), cutHair()
Alternative using different semaphore arrangement
Explain race condition if mutex not properly used
Explain customer loss when all chairs occupied
This is producer-consumer with bounded buffer of waiting chairs
